title: The Glove
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ PETER RONSARD loquitur.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Heigho!ן¿½ yawned one day King Francis,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Distance all value enhances!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When a manן¿½s busy, why, leisure
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Strikes him as wonderful pleasure,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Faith, and at leisure once is he?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Straightway he wants to be busy.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Here weן¿½ve got peace; and aghast Iן¿½m
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Caught thinking war the true pastime!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Is there a reason in metre?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Give us your speech, master Peter!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I who, if mortal dare say so,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Neן¿½er am at loss with my Naso,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sire,ן¿½ I replied, ן¿½joys prove cloudlets:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Men are the merest Ixionsן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here the King whistled aloud, ן¿½Letן¿½s
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ . . Heigho . . go look at our lions!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such are the sorrowful chances
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If you talk fine to King Francis.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so, to the courtyard proceeding,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our company, Francis was leading,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Increased by new followers tenfold
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Before he arrived at the penfold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lords, ladies, like clouds which bedizen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At sunset the western horizon.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Sir De Lorge pressed ן¿½mid the foremost
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the dame he professed to adore most.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, what a face! One by fits eyed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her, and the horrible pitside;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the penfold surrounded a hollow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which led where the eye scarce dared follow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And shelved to the chamber secluded
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where Bluebeard, the great lion, brooded.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The King bailed his keeper, an Arab
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As glossy and black as a scarab,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bade him make sport and at once stir
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Up and out of his den the old monster.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They opened a hole in the wire-work
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Across it, and dropped there a firework,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fled: oneן¿½s heartן¿½s beating redoubled;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A pause, while the pitן¿½s mouth was troubled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The blackness and silence so utter,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the fireworkן¿½s slow sparkling and sputter;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then earth in a sudden contortion
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gave out to our gaze her abortion!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such a brute! Were I friend Clement Marot
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Whose experience of natureן¿½s but narrow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And whose faculties move in no small mist
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he versifies David the Psalmist)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I should study that brute to describe you
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Illim Juda Leonem de Tribu!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oneן¿½s whole blood grew curdling and creepy
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To see the black mane, vast and heapy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The tail in the air stiff and straining,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wide eyes, nor waxing nor waning,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As over the barrier which bounded
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His platform, and us who surrounded
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The barrier, they reached and they rested
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On space that might stand him in best stead:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For who knew, he thought, what the amazement,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The eruption of clatter and blaze meant,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And if, in this minute of wonder,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No outlet, ן¿½mid lightning and thunder,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lay broad, and, his shackles all shivered,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lion at last was delivered?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ay, that was the open sky oן¿½erhead!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you saw by the flash on his forehead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the hope in those eyes wide and steady,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was leagues in the desert already,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Driving the flocks up the mountain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or catlike couched hard by the fountain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To waylay the date-gathering negress:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So guarded he entrance or egress.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How he stands!ן¿½ quoth the King: ן¿½we may well swear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (ן¿½No novice, weן¿½ve won our spurs elsewhere
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And so can afford the confession,)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We exercise wholesome discretion
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½In keeping aloof from his threshold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Once hold you, those jaws want no fresh hold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Their first would too pleasantly purloin
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The visitorן¿½s brisket or surloin:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But whoן¿½s he would prove so fool-hardy?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Not the best man of Marignan, pardie!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sentence no sooner was uttered,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than over the rails a glove flattered,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fell close to the lion, and rested:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dame ן¿½twas, who flung it and jested
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With life so, De Lorge had been wooing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For months past; he sate there pursuing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His suit, weighing out with nonchalance
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fine speeches like gold from a balance.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sound the trumpet, no true knightן¿½s a tarrier!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De Lorge made one leap at the barrier,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Walked straight to the glove, while the lion
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Neן¿½er moved, kept his far-reaching eye on
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The palm-tree-edged desert-springן¿½s sapphire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the musky oiled skin of the Kaffir,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Picked it up, and as calmly retreated,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leaped back where the lady was seated,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And full in the face of its owner
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flung the glove.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Your heartן¿½s queen, you dethrone her?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So should I!ן¿½ cried the King, ן¿½ן¿½twas mere vanity,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Not love, set that task to humanity!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lords and ladies alike turned with loathing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From such a proved wolf in sheepן¿½s clothing.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not so, I; for I caught an expression
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In her browן¿½s undisturbed self-possession
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Amid the Courtן¿½s scoffing and merriment,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if from no pleasing experiment
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She rose, yet of pain not much heedful
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So long as the process was needful,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if she had tried in a crucible,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To what ן¿½speeches like goldן¿½ were reducible,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, finding the finest prove copper,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Felt the smoke in her face was but proper;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To know what she had not to trust to,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was worth all the ashes and dust too.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She went out ן¿½mid hooting and laughter;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Clement Marot stayed; I followed after,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And asked, as a grace, what it all meant?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If she wished not the rash deedן¿½s recalment?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For Iן¿½, so I spoke, ן¿½am a poet:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Human nature, behoves that I know it!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She told me, ן¿½Too long had I heard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of the deed proved alone by the word:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For my love, what De Lorge would not dare!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With my scorn, what De Lorge could compare!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And the endless descriptions of death
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He would brave when my lip formed a breath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I must reckon as braved, or, of course,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Doubt his word, and moreover, perforce,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For such gifts as no lady could spurn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Must offer my love in return.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When I looked on your lion, it brought
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½All the dangers at once to my thought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Encountered by all sorts of men,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Before he was lodged in his den,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From the poor slave whose club or bare hands
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dug the trap, set the snare on the sands,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With no King and no Court to applaud,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½By no shame, should he shrink, overawed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yet to capture the creature made shift,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That his rude boys might laugh at the gift,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To the page who last leaped oן¿½er the fence
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of the pit, on no greater pretence
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Than to get back the bonnet he dropped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Lest his pay for a week should be stopped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So, wiser I judged it to make
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½One trial what ן¿½death for my sakeן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Really meant, while the power was yet mine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Than to wait until time should define
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Such a phrase not so simply as I,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who took it to mean just ן¿½to die.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The blow a glove gives is but weak:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Does the mark yet discolour my cheek?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But when the heart suffers a blow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Will the pain pass so soon, do you know?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I looked, as away she was sweeping,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And saw a youth eagerly keeping
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As close as he dared to the doorway:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No doubt that a noble should more weigh
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His life than befits a plebeian;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yet, had our brute been Nemean,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (I judge by a certain calm fervour
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The youth stepped with, forward to serve her)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d have scarce thought you did him the worst turn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If you whispered ן¿½Friend, what youן¿½d get, first earn!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when, shortly after, she carried
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her shame from the Court, and they married,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To that marriage some happiness, maugre
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The voice of the Court, I dared augur.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For De Lorge, he made women with men vie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Those in wonder and praise, these in envy;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in short stood so plain a head taller
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That he wooed and won . . . how do you call her?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The beauty, that rose in the sequel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the Kingן¿½s love, who loved her a week well.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½twas noticed he never would honour
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De Lorge (who looked daggers upon her)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the easy commission of stretching
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His legs in the service, and fetching
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His wife, from her chamber, those straying
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sad gloves she was always mislaying,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the King took the closet to chat in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But of course this adventure came pat in;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And never the King told the story,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How bringing a glove brought such glory,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the wife smiled, ן¿½His nerves are grown firmer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mine he brings now and utters no murmur.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Venienti occurrite morbo!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With which moral I drop my theorbo.
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
